Hello everyone! Today we will look at the partition() method in Python and exactly how it works.
The Core Concept
The partition() method takes a sequence of characters to search for as a parameter and splits the string into three parts. The first part is the segment of the string before the searched sequence, the second is the searched sequence itself, and the third is everything that comes after the searched sequence. As a result, the method returns a tuple containing these three parts of the string, meaning the tuple always has exactly 3 elements.
Essentially, this method first searches for the specified sequence and then splits the string into three parts based on where the searched sequence is located.
I also consider it necessary to note that the partition() method only considers the first occurrence of the searched sequence in the string. That is, it will not react to any subsequent occurrences of the specified sequence.
Usage in Code
First Example
First, let’s take the string “One, two, three” as an example:
text = "One, two, three"
text_parts = text.partition("two")
print(text_parts)('One, ', 'two', ', three')As we can see, we got exactly three parts of the string in a tuple according to the logic described earlier.
Second Example
Now let’s try changing the sequence parameter that needs to be found in the string. Let’s write “One” instead of “two”:
text = "One, two, three"
text_parts = text.partition("One")
print(text_parts)('', 'One', ', two, three')Looking at the result, you can see that even though the searched sequence “One” was located at the very beginning of the string, the method still divided the string into a nominal three parts and assigned an empty string as the first element (which represents the part before the searched sequence). Essentially, there was nothing before the word “One”, but this method is strictly obligated to split strings into exactly three parts, so it cannot do otherwise.
Third Example
Next, let’s try choosing a search sequence that isn’t in the string at all. For example, let’s choose the word “four”.
Here is the example:
text = "One, two, three"
text_parts = text.partition("four")
print(text_parts)('One, two, three', '', '')As we can see, the method did not find the searched sequence, so it simply placed the entire string into the first element of the tuple. Nevertheless, we still end up with exactly three elements in the tuple.
Conclusion
This article covered the partition() method in Python, which splits any string into three parts based on a specific principle.
Thank you all for your attention!
